turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the Court in two cases this morning.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The first opinion for the court I have is number 21-442, Reed versus Goertz. In 1996, a 19-year-old woman named Stacey Stites was brutally strangled to death and found by the side of the road in Bastrop County, Texas. The state charged Rodney Reed with the murder. At trial Reed claimed that he was innocent and that Stites' fiancé, Jimmy Fennell, or another acquaintance had committed the murder. The jury rejected the defense theory and found Reed guilty. Reed was sentenced to death. The Texas Court of Criminal Appeals affirmed the conviction and death sentence. Then in 2014, Reed filed a motion in state court under Texas' post-conviction DNA testing law. Reed requested DNA testing on more than 40 pieces of evidence including the belt used to strangle Stites. Reed contended that DNA testing would help identify the true perpetrator. The state trial court denied Reed's motion. The Court reasoned in part that many items Reed sought to test including the belt were not preserved through an adequate chain of custody. On appeal, the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals affirmed the trial court and later denied Reed's motion for rehearing. Reed next sued in federal court under Section 1983 of Title 42, asserting that Texas' post-conviction DNA testing law failed to provide procedural due process as required by the US Constitution. Among other things, Reed argued that Texas' stringent chain of custody requirement was unconstitutional. The US District Court for the Western District of Texas dismissed Reed's complaint. The US Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit affirmed that dismissal but did so on the ground that Reed's federal suit was filed too late after the two-year statute of limitations for such suits had run. The Fifth Circuit ruled that the statute of limitations began to run when the Texas trial court first denied Reed's DNA motion, which occurred more than two years before Reed filed his suit in federal court, not when the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals denied rehearing at the end of the state process. Reed then sought review in this Court. We granted review and now reverse the Fifth Circuit. The sole question before this Court at this time is whether Reed's federal lawsuit was timely. We conclude that it was timely. As a general matter, the statute of limitations begins to run when the plaintiff has a complete and present cause of action. Importantly, the Court has stated that a procedural due process claim such as Reeds is not complete when the deprivation occurs, rather the claim is complete only when the state fails to provide due process, and Texas' process for considering our request for DNA testing in capital cases includes not only the trial court proceedings, but also appellate review by the Court of Criminal Appeals, including a motion for rehearing. Therefore, Reed's federal 1983 claim was complete and the statute of limitations began to run only when the state litigation ended, that is when the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals denied Reed's motion for rehearing on his DNA testing claim. The soundness of that straightforward conclusion is reinforced by the consequences that would follow from a contrary approach that the statute of limitations for a 1983 suit like Reed's began to run immediately after a state trial court's denial of a motion for DNA testing as the Fifth Circuit concluded. The plaintiff would likely continue to pursue relief in the state system and simultaneously filed a protective federal suit challenging that ongoing state process. We see no good reason for such senseless duplication. Moreover, significant systemic benefits ensue from starting the statute of limitations clock only when the state litigation in DNA testing cases, like Reed's, has concluded. For example, if any due process flaws lurk in the DNA testing law, the state appellate process may cure those flaws thereby rendering a federal constitutional lawsuit unnecessary. In sum, when a prisoner pursues state post-conviction DNA testing through the state provided litigation process, the statute of limitations for a federal procedural due process claims begins to run only when the state litigation ends. In Reed's case, the statute of limitations began to run only when the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals denied Reed's motion for rehearing. Reed's federal lawsuit was therefore timely. For  those reasons, we reverse the judgment of the US Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Gorsuch joins.
